About.Name: Lucas Witthauer
About.AboutText: I'm an Electrical Engineering Major + Computer Engineering Minor and NCAA Division II athlete with a strong passion for problem-solving & technology.  Engineering has trained me to think analytically, work through complex systems, and build solutions through logic and creativity. I enjoy areas like circuit design, embedded systems, programming, and applied mathematics. Soccer has taught me discipline, leadership, and consistency. These skills directly translate into teamwork and performance in technical environments.  I'm always looking for opportunities where I can grow as an engineer, contribute to meaningful projects, and learn from strong teams. I take pride in being someone who shows up every day ready to work, improve, and compete at a high level!

Experience.ExperienceText: Explain your background and current focus.
Experience.EducationText: GPA: 3.36. Andy Grove Scholarship recipient. Relevant courses include Calculus 1–3, Linear Algebra, Differential Equations, Physics 1–3, Object-Oriented Programming, Data Structures, Computer Logic/Design, Circuits I–II, Computer Organization/Assembly Language, Electromagnetism, Electronics, and Microcontrollers.

Project1.Title: Bandpass Sallen Key IR Frequency Detector
Project1.Desc: This project involved designing and implementing an active Sallen-Key bandpass filter used to condition signals from an infrared detection system. The filter was designed to pass signals within a specific frequency range corresponding to the modulated infrared signal while attenuating unwanted noise and ambient interference. The circuit uses an operational amplifier with resistors and capacitors configured in the Sallen-Key topology to create the desired center frequency and bandwidth. The filtered output signal is then fed into a comparator, which converts the analog signal into a digital output by detecting when the signal exceeds a specified threshold. This allows the system to reliably detect the presence of the infrared signal while rejecting noise and other frequency components. This project demonstrates practical applications of active filtering, signal conditioning, and analog-to-digital interfacing in optical sensing systems..
